topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The spreadsheet glowed accusingly on the monitor. Sarah’s fingers hovered over the mouse, the cursor blinking an irritating, relentless rhythm. She had just finished cross-referencing the quarterly reports, and the disparity had become starkly apparent. Liam, the newest analyst, was clocking in significantly higher than she was, a fact that settled in her stomach like a cold stone. Every time a notification pinged from the payroll system, Sarah’s breath hitched. She forced herself to take a slow, deliberate sip of her lukewarm coffee, the bitter taste suddenly overwhelming. She needed to look busy, focused on the formatting of a benign document, anything to avoid meeting Liam’s gaze. The silence in the open-plan office seemed amplified, every muted keyboard click sounding like a gunshot in the sudden vacuum of her attention.

[story 2]

Mark shifted in his ergonomic chair, the leather squeaking in protest. He kept glancing at the screen, where the figures were laid out in stark, undeniable contrast. It felt like a physical weight pressing down on his chest, making it difficult to draw a full breath. He fiddled with the cuff of his shirt, tugging at the fabric as if to physically anchor himself. When his supervisor approached his desk, Mark managed a tight, almost strained smile. “Everything alright, Mark?” the supervisor asked, their tone perfectly neutral. Mark swallowed hard, his throat suddenly dry. “Fine, just… reviewing some figures,” he managed, his voice sounding unnaturally high. He felt a desperate urge to look anywhere but at the numbers, to focus intensely on a smudge on his desk blotter, anywhere but the reality of the compensation gap.

[story 3]

Elena reread the email three times, her eyes darting back and forth across the text. The numbers were undeniable, a sharp spike in her internal equilibrium. A tremor ran through her hands, causing her to grip the edge of her desk so tightly her knuckles turned white. She tried to concentrate on the ambient hum of the ventilation system, using the mechanical drone as a shield against the startling clarity of the situation. When her colleague, David, walked past, Elena instinctively hunched her shoulders, trying to shrink into her workspace. She offered a quick, clipped nod, her voice barely a whisper. “Morning,” she murmured, the sound catching slightly in her throat. She waited, holding her posture rigid, praying that David wouldn't notice the subtle stiffness radiating from her.
